title: ImpEx — Import, Export & Data Migration
description: SAP Commerce Cloud's declarative CSV-based format for importing, exporting, and migrating platform data at any scale.

sections.title: What is ImpEx?
sections.content:
ImpEx (Import/Export) is SAP Commerce Cloud's built-in mechanism for creating, updating, removing, and exporting data using a declarative CSV-like syntax. It is one of the most frequently used tools in day-to-day Commerce Cloud development and is heavily tested in the certification exam.

ImpEx scripts are plain text files with a .impex extension. They are used for:

  • Essential data — mandatory data required for the extension to function (loaded during Initialize and Update System)
  • Project data — sample or demo data loaded during initialization
  • Data migrations — one-off scripts run during upgrades to transform existing data
  • Test data setup — creating fixtures for automated tests

ImpEx scripts can be executed from three places: the HAC ImpEx console (Console → ImpEx Import), programmatically via the ImportService Spring bean, or automatically during system initialization from an extension's resources/impex/ folder.

Where ImpEx fits: Think of ImpEx as the data layer equivalent of SQL scripts, but type-aware and integrated with the TypeSystem. It respects interceptors, validates types, and operates through the ServiceLayer — it is not a raw database operation.

sections.title: Basic ImpEx Syntax — INSERT, UPDATE, INSERT_UPDATE, REMOVE
sections.content:
Every ImpEx script consists of one or more header lines followed by data lines. The header defines the operation, the type, and the attribute columns. Data lines provide the values.

The Four Operations

  • INSERT — creates new items. Fails if an item with the same unique key already exists.
  • UPDATE — updates existing items. Fails if the item does not exist.
  • INSERT_UPDATE — creates the item if it does not exist, updates it if it does. The safest and most commonly used operation.
  • REMOVE — deletes items matching the given unique key.

Basic Example

INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; name[lang=en]; catalogVersion(catalog(id), version)
                     ; LAPTOP-001       ; Gaming Laptop ; electronicsProductCatalog:Staged
                     ; LAPTOP-002       ; Office Laptop ; electronicsProductCatalog:Staged

Breaking down the header:

  • INSERT_UPDATE Product — operation and type
  • code[unique=true] — the code attribute is the unique identifier used to look up existing items
  • name[lang=en] — sets the localized name attribute for English
  • catalogVersion(catalog(id), version) — a reference expression resolving a CatalogVersion by its catalog's id and version string
Column separator: ImpEx uses semicolons (;) as column separators by default, not commas. The first ; on a data line is the separator before the first value column. Leading and trailing whitespace around values is trimmed automatically.

sections.title: References, Modifiers and Special Syntax
sections.content:
ImpEx's power comes from its ability to resolve references between types and apply modifiers that control import behavior.

Reference Resolution

To set a relation attribute, ImpEx resolves references using parenthetical expressions that navigate the type graph:

; productCode ; category(code)      ; unit(code)
; LAPTOP-001  ; electronics         ; pieces

The expression category(code) means: find a Category whose code attribute matches the given value. You can chain multiple levels deep:

; catalogVersion(catalog(id), version)
; myProductCatalog:Online

This resolves a CatalogVersion by first finding a Catalog with the given id, then finding the version within it.

Key Modifiers

  • [unique=true] — marks the attribute as part of the unique key used to look up existing items for UPDATE/INSERT_UPDATE
  • [lang=en] — sets the value for a specific language on a localized attribute
  • [default=someValue] — uses this value if the data line's cell is empty
  • [virtual=true] — the column is not mapped to a type attribute; used with translators or scripts
  • [allownull=true] — allows writing a null value, overriding the platform's null-check
  • [forceWrite=true] — forces writing even to read-only attributes
INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; name[lang=en][default=Unnamed]; price[allownull=true]
                     ; PROD-001         ;                               ;
Multiple unique keys: You can mark multiple columns as [unique=true]. The combination of all unique columns forms the composite key used for lookup. This is how you uniquely identify items that have no single natural key.

sections.title: Macros, Collections and Multi-value Attributes
sections.content:
ImpEx provides several features that make large scripts more maintainable and expressive.

Macros

Macros are reusable substitutions defined at the top of an ImpEx file. They reduce repetition for frequently used values like catalog versions:

$catalog=electronicsProductCatalog
$catalogVersion=$catalog:Online
$contentCatalog=electronicsContentCatalog

INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; catalogVersion(catalog(id),version)
                     ; LAPTOP-001       ; $catalogVersion
                     ; LAPTOP-002       ; $catalogVersion

Macros are simple text substitutions — the $macro token is replaced literally before parsing. They are not typed variables.

Collections and Multi-value Attributes

For attributes that accept multiple values (collections or many-to-many relations), separate values with a comma inside the cell:

INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; supercategories(code)
                     ; LAPTOP-001       ; electronics,computers,laptops

The default collection delimiter is a comma. You can change it with the [collection-delimiter=|] modifier if values contain commas.

Scripting with BeanShell

ImpEx supports embedded BeanShell scripts for dynamic value computation at import time. Scripts are prefixed with #%:

#% impex.setTargetFile( "my-export.csv" );

INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; name[lang=en]
                     ; #% java.util.UUID.randomUUID().toString() ; Dynamic Product
BeanShell scope: BeanShell scripts in ImpEx run in the context of the import session and have access to the impex variable (the ImpEx session object). This is powerful but can make scripts hard to debug — use it sparingly and only when macros or modifiers cannot solve the problem.

sections.title: Essential Data, Project Data and Automatic Loading
sections.content:
ImpEx scripts inside an extension's resources/impex/ folder are loaded automatically during system initialization and update, following a specific naming convention.

File Naming Convention

  • essentialdata-*.impex — loaded during both Initialize and Update System. Used for data the system cannot function without (e.g. user groups, base sites, currencies).
  • projectdata-*.impex — loaded only during Initialize (full reset). Used for sample or demo data that should not be re-applied to production.
resources/
└── impex/
    ├── essentialdata-sites.impex
    ├── essentialdata-currencies.impex
    ├── projectdata-sampleproducts.impex
    └── projectdata-samplecategories.impex

Loading Order

ImpEx files are loaded in extension dependency order. An extension that depends on another will have its ImpEx files loaded after the dependency's files. Within a single extension, files are loaded alphabetically. This matters when one script references data created by another.

Programmatic Import

To run an ImpEx script programmatically from Java:

@Autowired
private ImportService importService;

public void runImport(String scriptContent) {
    ImportConfig config = new ImportConfig();
    config.setScript(new StreamBasedImpExResource(
        new ByteArrayInputStream(
            scriptContent.getBytes()), "UTF-8"));
    config.setFailOnError(true);
    ImportResult result = importService.importData(config);
    if (result.hasUnresolvedLines()) {
        // Handle errors
    }
}
Unresolved lines: When an ImpEx import encounters a line it cannot process (e.g. a reference that does not resolve), it marks it as an unresolved line and continues by default. Always check result.hasUnresolvedLines() after programmatic imports and log or handle them — silent failures are a common source of missing data bugs.

sections.title: ImpEx Export and the HAC Console
sections.content:
ImpEx is not only for import — it can also export data from the platform into a script that can be re-imported elsewhere. This is useful for moving data between environments (e.g. from production to staging) or for creating data snapshots.

Export Syntax

EXPORT Product WHERE {catalogVersion} = ?catalogVersion
SELECT {code}, {name[lang=en]}, {catalogVersion}
FROM {Product}
WHERE {catalogVersion} IN
    ({{ SELECT {pk} FROM {CatalogVersion}
       WHERE {version} = 'Online' }})

The double curly braces {{ }} denote an inline subquery — a FlexibleSearch query embedded inside an ImpEx expression. This is one of the most powerful (and most exam-tested) ImpEx features.

The HAC ImpEx Console

The HAC (Hybris Administration Console) provides an interactive ImpEx console at Console → ImpEx Import and Console → ImpEx Export. Key features:

  • Paste and run ImpEx scripts interactively
  • View unresolved lines and error details
  • Upload .impex files for execution
  • Toggle validation mode to check the script without actually committing changes

Validation Mode

Running an import in validation mode checks the script for syntax errors and unresolvable references without writing any data. This is invaluable before running a migration script on a production system.

Inline FlexibleSearch {{ }}: Double curly braces embed a FlexibleSearch query inside an ImpEx value cell. The query must return a single column of PKs. This allows you to resolve complex references that cannot be expressed with simple parenthetical navigation. It is one of the most frequently tested ImpEx advanced features.

questions.text: Which ImpEx operation creates an item if it does not exist and updates it if it does?
options: INSERT
UPDATE
INSERT_UPDATE
UPSERT
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. INSERT_UPDATE is the safest and most commonly used operation — it performs an upsert. INSERT fails if the item already exists, and UPDATE fails if it does not. There is no UPSERT keyword in ImpEx.

questions.text: ImpEx files named 'projectdata-*.impex' are loaded during both Initialize System and Update System operations.
questions.explanation: False. projectdata-*.impex files are loaded only during Initialize System (full reset). essentialdata-*.impex files are loaded during both Initialize and Update System. This distinction prevents sample data from being re-applied to production environments on every update.

questions.text: In this ImpEx header: 'INSERT_UPDATE Product; code[unique=true]; catalogVersion(catalog(id),version)' — what does the expression 'catalogVersion(catalog(id),version)' do?
options: It sets the catalogVersion attribute to the literal string 'catalog(id),version'
It resolves a CatalogVersion by navigating to a Catalog by its id and then finding the matching version within it
It creates a new CatalogVersion if one does not exist with the given id and version
It defines a virtual column that is not mapped to any type attribute
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Parenthetical reference expressions in ImpEx navigate the type graph to resolve related items. catalogVersion(catalog(id),version) means: find a CatalogVersion whose parent Catalog has the given id AND whose version string matches. The data cell value would be formatted as myCatalog:Online.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the [unique=true] modifier on an ImpEx column?
options: It enforces a database UNIQUE constraint on that column
It marks the attribute as the identifier used to look up existing items for UPDATE and INSERT_UPDATE operations
It prevents duplicate values from appearing in the same import file
It makes the column required — the import fails if the cell is empty
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. [unique=true] designates a column as part of the lookup key. For UPDATE and INSERT_UPDATE, the platform uses the unique column(s) to find the existing item before deciding whether to insert or update. Multiple columns can be marked unique, forming a composite key.

questions.text: ImpEx macros defined with '$macroName=value' are type-safe variables that the platform validates before executing the script.
questions.explanation: False. ImpEx macros are simple text substitutions — the token $macroName is replaced with its defined string before parsing. There is no type-checking or validation of macro values. An incorrect macro value only causes an error when the substituted line is processed.

questions.text: What do double curly braces {{ }} denote inside an ImpEx script?
options: A BeanShell script block for dynamic value computation
A macro definition block
An inline FlexibleSearch subquery used to resolve references
A comment block that is ignored during processing
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Double curly braces embed a FlexibleSearch query inline within an ImpEx expression. The subquery must return a single column of PKs and is used to resolve complex references that cannot be expressed with simple parenthetical navigation. BeanShell scripts use the #% prefix instead.

questions.text: After a programmatic ImpEx import via ImportService, the result object indicates there are unresolved lines. What does this mean?
options: The import failed completely and no data was written to the database
Some lines could not be processed (e.g. unresolvable references) and were skipped; other lines may have been committed
The import is queued and will be retried automatically by the platform
Unresolved lines indicate a syntax error in the ImpEx header — no data lines were processed
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. By default, ImpEx skips lines it cannot resolve and continues processing. Unresolved lines indicate partial failures — some data may have been written while other lines were skipped. Always check and handle result.hasUnresolvedLines() after programmatic imports to avoid silent data gaps.

questions.text: ImpEx operations respect ValidateInterceptors — a ValidateInterceptor that blocks a save will also block the corresponding ImpEx line.
questions.explanation: True. ImpEx operates through the ServiceLayer and ModelService, not raw SQL. This means all interceptors — including ValidateInterceptor — fire during ImpEx imports. A validation rule that blocks a save in Java code will equally block the same data when imported via ImpEx.

questions.text: In which order are ImpEx files loaded when multiple extensions are active?
options: Alphabetically by filename across all extensions simultaneously
In extension dependency order — dependent extensions load their ImpEx after their dependencies; alphabetically within each extension
Randomly — ImpEx loading order is not guaranteed
In reverse dependency order — the most dependent extensions load first
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. ImpEx files load in extension dependency order, ensuring that data created by a dependency's scripts is available when the dependent extension's scripts run. Within a single extension, files load alphabetically. This ordering is critical when scripts reference data created by other scripts.

questions.text: How do you set the English name of a product using a localized attribute in ImpEx?
options: ; PROD-001 ; name = 'English Name'
Use a separate header column: name[lang=en]
Use a BeanShell script to set the localization at runtime
Localized attributes cannot be set via ImpEx — use the Backoffice instead
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Localized attributes are set in ImpEx by appending the [lang=languageCode] modifier to the column header. For example: name[lang=en] sets the English name, and you can have multiple language columns in the same header: name[lang=en]; name[lang=de].

questions.text: Running an ImpEx import in validation mode writes data to the database but rolls it back at the end, making it safe to test scripts on production.
questions.explanation: False. Validation mode checks syntax and reference resolution without writing any data to the database at all — it is not a rollback mechanism. No data is written during validation mode, making it safe to use on any environment including production to verify a script before executing it for real.

questions.text: A product has multiple categories. How do you assign all three categories in a single ImpEx data line?
options: Use three separate data lines, one per category
Separate the category codes with a comma inside the cell: ; electronics,computers,laptops
Use the [multi=true] modifier and separate values with a pipe character
Multi-value attributes require a separate JOIN table ImpEx — they cannot be set on the main type header
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. ImpEx uses comma as the default collection delimiter for multi-value attributes. Multiple values are placed in a single cell separated by commas. If values themselves contain commas, use the [collection-delimiter=|] modifier to change the separator to a different character.
